coredata.dc:description: Protein aggregation is correlated with the onset and progression of protein misfolding diseases (PMDs). Inhibiting the generation of toxic aggregates of misfolded proteins has been proposed as a therapeutic approach for PMDs. Due to their unique properties, nanomaterials have been extensively investigated for their ability to inhibit protein aggregation and have shown great potential in the diagnosis and treatment of PMDs. However, the precise mechanisms by which nanomaterials interact with amyloidogenic proteins and the factors influencing these interactions remain poorly understood. Consequently, developing a rational design strategy for nanomaterials that target specific proteins in PMDs has been challenging. In this review, we elucidate the effects of nanomaterials on protein aggregation and describe the mechanisms through which nanomaterials interfere with protein aggregation. The major factors impacting protein-nanomaterial interaction such as size, charge, concentration, surface modification and morphology that can be rationally addressed to achieve the desired effects of nanomaterials on protein aggregation are summarized. The prospects and challenges to the clinical application of nanomaterials for the treatment of PMDs are also discussed.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The aggregation process and mechanisms of cytotoxicity of amyloidogenic proteins. (A) The aggregation process is typically divided into three stages: a nucleation phase, an elongation phase and a plateau phase. (B) The aggregates cause cell damage through lipid membrane permeabilization, endoplasmic reticulum stress, mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress.

Structure and classification of nanomaterials. (A) Nanomaterials are usually composed of a core material that sometimes has surface modifiers; (B) Based on the core structure of nanomaterials they can be divided into organic nanomaterials, inorganic nanomaterials and carbon nanomaterials.

Mechanisms by which nanomaterials disrupt protein aggregation. The mechanisms nanomaterials employ to affect amyloid aggregation may include: affecting native protein structures, disrupting the formation of β-sheet rich aggregates, blocking the elongation of oligomeric intermediates, disaggregation of preformed fibrils and changing protein distribution. (Aβ42 Amyloid Fibrils PDB ID: 5KK3).

Factors that impact the effects of nanomaterials on protein aggregation. (A) Nanomaterials of different sizes show different impacts on protein aggregation. Small nanomaterials usually show stronger inhibitory effects. (B) Effects of nanomaterial-protein concentration ratio on protein aggregation. Low nanomaterial-protein ratio leads to high surface protein concentration, thus accelerating protein aggregation; high nanomaterial-protein ratio will decrease the surface protein concentration and inhibit protein aggregation. Human prion protein (PDB ID:1QLX) (C) Effects of the surface electric charge of nanomaterials on protein aggregation. Most amyloidogenic proteins contain charged aggregation core region, which can be recognized and bound by charged nanomaterials. Bovine serum albumin (PDB ID: 4F5S) (D) Surface modifiers of nanomaterials induce different effects on protein aggregation. (E) Effects of morphology and surface roughness of nanomaterials on protein aggregation. Compared to smooth spherical surfaces, amyloidogenic proteins are more likely to be absorbed on irregularly shaped rough surfaces. Hen Egg White Lysozyme (PDB ID: 7D0W).
Effects of nanomaterials on protein aggregation.

head.ce:title: Inhibiting protein aggregation with nanomaterials: The underlying mechanisms and impact factors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Protein aggregation is correlated with the onset and progression of protein misfolding diseases (PMDs). Inhibiting the generation of toxic aggregates of misfolded proteins has been proposed as a therapeutic approach for PMDs. Due to their unique properties, nanomaterials have been extensively investigated for their ability to inhibit protein aggregation and have shown great potential in the diagnosis and treatment of PMDs. However, the precise mechanisms by which nanomaterials interact with amyloidogenic proteins and the factors influencing these interactions remain poorly understood. Consequently, developing a rational design strategy for nanomaterials that target specific proteins in PMDs has been challenging. In this review, we elucidate the effects of nanomaterials on protein aggregation and describe the mechanisms through which nanomaterials interfere with protein aggregation. The major factors impacting protein-nanomaterial interaction such as size, charge, concentration, surface modification and morphology that can be rationally addressed to achieve the desired effects of nanomaterials on protein aggregation are summarized. The prospects and challenges to the clinical application of nanomaterials for the treatment of PMDs are also discussed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protein misfolding diseases and protein aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: A broad range of human diseases arise from the failure of specific proteins to maintain their native functional conformation. These pathological conditions are generally referred to as protein misfolding diseases (PMDs) [1,2]. The main clinical feature of PMDs is the presence of amyloid deposits in specific tissues/organs that are composed of amyloidogenic proteins/peptides, such as amyloid β (Aβ) in Alzheimer's disease (AD) [3,4], α-Synuclein (α-Syn) in Parkinson's disease (PD) [5,6], huntingtin protein (HTT) in Huntington's disease (HD) [7], transactive response DNA binding protein 43 (TDP-43) in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis and frontotemporal lobar degeneration (ALS and FTLD respectively) [8,9], islet amyloid polypeptide (IAPP) in type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) [10], and the prion protein in transmissible spongiform encephalopathies (TSE; prion diseases) [11]. In many cases, the misfolded protein aggregates form amyloid.

Under pathological conditions, amyloidogenic proteins form fibrils with similar biophysical properties and share alike aggregation kinetics, despite their differences in sequences and sizes [1,12–14]. The aggregation process is usually divided into three stages, nucleation, elongation and plateau [15] (Fig. 1A). In the initial nucleation stage, native folded proteins usually cross energy barriers to unfold before transforming into β-sheet-rich oligomers/protofibrils [16]. Post the nucleation phase, monomers continuously attach to the nuclei to form long and mature fibrils in the elongation and plateau phases. In amyloidogenic proteins, three crucial blocks with aggregation-regulating potentials are commonly observed, including regions with high fibrillation propensity, α-helical structure, and intramolecular SS bond [17]. Hence, interfering these blocks may be explored to inhibit aggregation.

β-sheet rich oligomers primarily formed in nucleation stage are regarded as the major toxic species during fibrillation and have shown adverse impacts on cells [18]. Therefore, reducing the production of oligomers has been considered as a key estimation indicator of aggregation and possible unfavorable pathology. The intermediate oligomers produced during aggregation cause damage to cells by different mechanisms, such as lipid membrane permeabilization, endoplasmic reticulum stress, mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress (Fig. 1B) [12,19]. Therefore, inhibiting the generation of toxic protein aggregates with antibodies, peptide/protein mimics, small molecules or nanomaterials have all been proposed as therapeutic approaches for preventing/treating PMDs [19–25]. Of these, nanomaterials are attracting an increasing great deal amount of attention due to their many unique properties (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials in the treatment of PMDs

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials are naturally or artificially synthesized materials with sub-micrometer scales (1–100 nm) and possess strong adsorption capacities, high surface area to volume ratios, and additional unique physical properties [26,27]. Generally, nanomaterials are composed of a core material and surface modifiers, which endows them with physicochemical properties such as charge, hydrophobicity, or other surface chemical properties [28]. Surface modifiers may be specific chemical compounds, peptides or proteins (Fig. 2A) [29]. Based on their chemical compositions, nanomaterials can be divided into organic nanomaterials mainly comprised of lipids, proteins, polysaccharides and organic macromolecule polymers [30,31]; inorganic nanomaterials with metal and/or metal oxide constituents [32]; and carbon nanomaterials that are exclusively composed of carbon (Fig. 2B) [33,34].

As a result of their multiple advantages over conventional drugs [35], nanomaterials are increasingly used as biomedicines. From the physical and chemical perspectives [27], their high surface to volume ratio and good physical/chemical stability endows nanomaterials prolonged drug release, increased aqueous solubility of otherwise insoluble drugs, and great capacity for carrying both lipophilic and hydrophilic drugs [36]. Biologically, nanomaterials exhibit excellent permeability, bioavailability and biocompatibility [37]. Therefore, nanomaterials have shown significant potentials for the diagnosis/treatment of cancer and neurodegenerative diseases [38–40].

Many drug candidates fail to cross the blood-brain barrier (BBB) [41,42], whereas, due to their size/surface polarity, controllable nanomaterials are great candidates for carrying drugs that target the brain [43]. Moreover, the high surface to volume ratio, tunable surface charge and hydrophobicity make nanomaterials effective inhibitors of protein aggregation [44,45]. Studies have revealed a variety of mechanisms by which nanomaterials attenuate aggregation or disaggregate preformed fibrils/aggregates [46–49]. Herein, we introduce recent advances in the study of nanomaterials aimed at preventing aggregation, as well as the mechanisms and factors that underly their efficacy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms by which nanomaterials disrupt protein aggregation

ce:section.ce:para: There are two main mechanisms by which nanomaterials affect native protein structures to interfere with aggregation, stabilization, and perturbation. For most aggregation prone proteins, exposing buried hydrophobic cores and backbone hydrogen-bond donors/acceptors is believed to be the initial step in aggregation [50,51]; the exposure of hydrophobic patches can change amyloidogenicity of proteins as well as the structure or toxicity of fibrils [52,53]. In the folding process, the covalent bonds including disulfide bonds are essential for the stability of proteins and formation of amyloid fibrils [54,55]. On one hand, adsorption of proteins through hydrogen bonds and/or van der Waals forces may stabilize the native random coil/α-helix rich secondary structures and minimize the exposure of hydrophobic cores, leading to extended lag time. For example, using circular dichroism spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and molecular dynamics stimulation studies, the binding between carbon nanoparticles and human insulin has been demonstrated to stabilize monomeric insulin and prevent its conformational conversion [56]. Similar findings have been reported in other nanomaterials against amyloidogenic protein aggregation, such as AuNPs for lysozyme [57], carbon dots for hIAPP [58], and silica nanoparticles for human γD-crystallin [59].

On the other hand, on the surface of nanomaterials, refolding and unfolding of protein structures may take place with different consequences. For example, human serum albumin nanoparticles facilitate unfolding of hen egg white lysozyme (HEWL) which induces exposure of hydrophobic cores of HEWL as measured by ANS fluorescence spectroscopy; the unfolding results in formation of more flexible secondary structures, which consequently favors protein-nanoparticle interaction rather than protein-protein aggregation [60,61]. Whereas in another study, induced unfolding of bovine serum albumin (BSA) on the surface of gold nanorods was suggested by dynamic light scattering and fluorescence decay measurements, and the unfolded BSA was prone to interact with native/unfolded proteins to form large amorphous aggregates [62]. Mechanistically, globular proteins usually contain a hydrophobic core, and hydrophilic external regions available for Van der Waals forces. In nanomaterials-protein interactions, electrostatic repulsion and hydrogen bonding play crucial roles, mostly at the nucleation stage of aggregation, in which functional groups (e.g., -OH and -NH2) on the surface of nanoparticles inhibit the formation of oligomerization and misfolding via electronic repulsion [63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Disrupting the formation of β-sheet rich aggregates
ce:section.ce:para: During fibrillation, the formation of cross-β structures and continuous conversion of oligomers to fibrils determines the morphology and toxicity of fibrils. Nanomaterials prevent the formation of β-structures through multiple mechanisms. Some can induce proteins to form random coils instead of β-sheet rich aggregates [64]; or they may cause off-pathway aggregation by turning proteins into amyloids that lack typical fibrillar morphology [65]. Furthermore, certain nanomaterials, e.g., silica nanoparticles, induce unconventional aggregation that does not produce toxic oligomeric intermediates and typical β-sheet conformation [66]. In light of oligomers as major toxic intermediates during fibrillation both in vitro and in vivo [18], nanomaterials such as chiral silica nanoribbons could be designed to reduce the generation of toxic oligomers [67], which provides a new angle for inhibiting cytotoxic amyloidosis. Interestingly, some nanomaterials inhibit fibrillation through disrupting newly formed β-sheet structures. For example, multifunctional carbon dots break β-sheet configuration by interrupting the intermolecular β-sheet stacking that occurs in copper-mediated Aβ aggregation; researchers proposed that nanomaterials could act as β-sheet breaker to interfere with crucial bonds for β-sheet stacking [68].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Blocking the elongation of oligomeric intermediates
ce:section.ce:para: Some nanomaterials block the elongation of oligomeric intermediates. Nanomaterials may trap monomeric or oligomeric proteins like nanofluid or nanocage, and block the hydrophobic protein-protein interactions to inhibit further aggregation [69]. Moreover, intermolecular interactions between nanomaterials and aggregating monomers/oligomers, such as enhanced hydrogen bonding, may also block active elongation sites on oligomers/protofibrils at the nucleation/elongation stage [70,71]. For example, AuNPs have been reported to stabilize the monomeric and dimeric forms of β-lactoglobulin, thus inhibiting the nucleation and elongation phase via forming nanocage, blocking intramolecular and intermolecular disulfide bond exchange between peptides as well as hydrophobic interactions necessary for fibrillation [69]. In another study, nitrogen-doping bifunctional carbon dots (N-BCDs) block the aggregation of human insulin oligomers and prevent protofibrils growth even after the completion of nucleation; insulin monomers prefer interaction with N-BCDs rather than with insulin oligomers, thus blocking elongation [71].

Some nanomaterials disaggregate preformed fibrils through two generally acknowledged ways, photothermal ablation and physicochemical property-induced disaggregation. Photothermal therapy, characterized by high selectivity and minimal invasiveness, is a biomedical approach targeting tumors or bacteria [72]. Nanomaterials, mostly noble metallic nanoparticles and carbon nanoparticles, have been used as photothermal agents due to their unique surface plasmon resonance properties and capacity to absorb light of different wavelengths [72–74]. When exposed to light irradiation, these metallic or carbon nanomaterials absorb near-infrared light to create a hotspot and/or generate oxidative stress which facilitates the fragmentation or dissolution of fibrils [71,72,75], thus disintegrating mature amyloid fibrils or inhibiting fibrillation [76–78]. Graphene oxide conjugated with thioflavin S (ThS) exhibits strong binding for Aβ fibrils, and upon laser irradiation at 808 nm, it effectively dissociates amyloid deposits both in buffer and in murine cerebrospinal fluid [77]. Most photothermal ablation based anti-amyloid researches have been done in in vitro studies. However, this technology which was originally applied in cancer treatment causes cell damage due to overheating accompanied by ROS release [74], necessitating extensive investigation before it can be safely applied against protein misfolding diseases.

Moreover, the capacity to disaggregate preformed fibrils depends on the surface morphology, intrinsic stability, as well as the composition of nanomaterials. Nanomaterials with specifically designed morphologies, such as nanoplates [79], nanospheres [79], nanorods [80], nanoflowers [81], have shown a great capacity in inhibiting aggregation. Analysis of these structures by electron microscopy analysis suggests that these specific morphologies allow nanomaterials to cleave long fibrils into short and ragged pieces, therefore resulting in decreased cytotoxicity [79]. Due to their sharp edges and larger surface area, ZnO nanoflowers are more effective in inhibiting aggregation than ZnO nanospheres [81]. For ZnO nanospheres, a docking simulation study suggested these nanoparticles could fit in the cavity formed by hexameric human insulin and thus stabilizing the structure of insulin hexamers, and Ser9, His10, and Glu13 of insulin contribute to these interactions [81]. However, ZnO nanoflowers, with higher surface area to volume ratio due to flower petals, associate with more solvent to form a larger hydrodynamic diameter, hence show stronger anti-amyloid capacity compared to the spherical counterparts [82].

It has been demonstrated that interfacial interaction plays a key role in regulating the transformation of amorphous aggregates to ordered aggregates in conventional aggregation process [83]. By developing a macromolecular mesocrystal, the macromolecular fibrillation pathway is affected [84]. Therefore, adsorption of proteins on nanomaterials forms a complex which may alter interfacial interaction to affect fibrillation. Due to the high surface area to volume ratios and surface charges of nanomaterials, many protein molecules are nonspecifically adsorbed to their surface through hydrogen bonding or electrostatic attraction; the functional groups or ligands of the nanomaterials also bind hydrophobic patches in protein through covalent bonds.

These bindings affect protein distribution in solution and fibrillation differently. Surface absorption causes a relatively high local concentration of proteins on the surface of nanomaterials. In some cases, the nanomaterial works as a conventional catalyst to facilitate nuclei/protofibril oligomerization and fibril elongation [85,86], such as the amyloid-promoting effects of gold nanoparticles on α-Syn fibrillation [86], gold nanospheres and nanotubes on Aβ fibrillation [87]. While in other cases, by trapping monomeric proteins on the surface of nanomaterials, the number of critical nuclei formed during early stages of fibrillation is reduced retarding amyloid formation. For example, amino modified polystyrene nanoparticles adsorb monomeric Aβ peptides onto their surface, then the adsorbed proteins spread thinly on the surface of NPs rather than oligomerizing or nucleating, thus decreasing the concentration of proteins in solution, and increasing the lag time of fibril formation [88,89]. Analogous studies demonstrated that adsorption of HEWL on the surface of ZnO NPs contributed to the depletion of free protein in media, leading to a prolonged lag phase and elongation phase and inhibiting fibrillation [90]. Which of the two scenarios dominates depends on many factors, e.g., peptide to nanoparticle surface area ratio [91]; therefore, for different NPs and amyloidogenic proteins, there should exist a critical balance point between two pathways, which needs to be carefully considered in the development of amyloidogenic nano-inhibitors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors that impact the effects of nanomaterials on protein aggregation

ce:section.ce:para: The sizes of nanomaterials, which are reversely correlated with their curvatures (Fig. 4A), play important roles in affecting amyloid aggregation. Nanoparticles and non-spherical nanomaterials all show strong curvature-dependent adsorption [92,93]. For instance, for Au nanoparticles of larger size (36 and 18 nm), their small surface curvature provides a relatively flat surface, which strengthens adsorption and increases surface local protein concentration to accelerate aggregation [26]; whereas smaller sized Au nanoparticles (6 nm) tend to form complexes with protein monomers and dispersed in the system, thereby attenuating aggregation [60,94]. However, opposite effects on amyloid formation were observed for other nanomaterials. For example, bigger iron oxide nanoparticles can also inhibit aggregation by preventing the formation of mature fibrils [95]. These seemingly contrary results reflect the complex influencing factors on amyloid formation.

The surface charge of nanomaterials is another important factor affecting protein aggregation [79,88,94,96–103]. Through monitoring the zeta potential of a solution and its change over time, the impact of charged nanomaterials on protein aggregation has been investigated. Differently charged Au nanoparticles affect amylin fibrillation differently: positively-charged nanoparticles inhibit amylin aggregation while negatively-charged nanoparticles neutralize the net charge of amylin and promote aggregation [98]. This suggests that the net-charge of nanomaterials can significantly impact protein structures through electrostatic interactions further affecting protein aggregation (Fig. 4C) [98,104–106]. For example, the negatively charged Aβ40 has a strong electrostatic interaction with positively-charged gold nanoparticles; this interaction limits the flexibility of the Aβ40 structure required for Aβ40 to undergo a conformational transition and retards Aβ40 aggregation [26]. Similarly, the strong electrostatic interaction between chitosan-hyaluronic acid composite NPs (CH NPs) and Aβ40 inhibit Aβ40 aggregation [97]. Specifically, positively-charged CH NPs are bound to Aβ40 through electrostatic attraction, thereby effectively inhibiting Aβ40 nucleation and fibrillation; while the negatively-charged CH NPs only bind two positively-charged residues (Lys16 and Lys28) of Aβ40 to inhibit the conformational transition of Aβ40 [97]. Therefore, in-depth understanding of the effects of nanomaterial surface charge on protein aggregation can provide the insights needed for the rational design of nanochips/nano-inhibitors for PMDs.

The importance of amphiphilic surface chemistry and polar groups to form hydrogen bonds have been demonstrated in inhibiting aggregation. For examples, in the presence of amphiphilic graphene quantum dots, hIAPP fibrillation generated much less toxic oligomeric intermediates [107], even aberrant protein expressions caused by hIAPP fibrillation were remarkably rescued [108]. Likewise, amphiphilic fullerenols displayed significant inhibition on α-Syn aggregation compared to hydrophobic fullerenes [109]. Based on these findings, nanoparticle surfaces could be differently modified to enhance the inhibitory performance.

Modifiers can be used to change the surface properties of nanomaterials to affect the interaction between nanomaterials and proteins. Generally, surface modification of nanomaterials can be divided into three categories: thiols, amines, and hydroxyls that are generally used to anchor ligands to the NPs surfaces [29,110–114]. For example, different surface modifications [citric acid, cysteine, polyethylene glycol (PEG) and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB)] generate Au nanoparticles with different sizes, surface charges and surfaces roughness that confers different effects on the conformation of bovine serum albumin [115]. In addition, many small molecule or peptide inhibitors exhibit excellent inhibition effects in vitro, however, due to their lack of targeting effects or short biological half-life, only a few have achieved significant results in vivo [12,116,117]. Addition of these small molecule or peptide inhibitors to the surface of nanomaterials would effectively enhance their targeting and/or prolong their half-lives [111,118,119]. Amyloid-like protein assembly on nanoparticle surface is a general approach to functionalize different nanoparticles. For instance, by using a lysozyme-containing transition buffer, the amyloid-like assembly could be utilized to functionalize the surface of a variety of nanomaterials, including inorganic, polymeric or metal materials, in a convenient and biocompatible manner [120]. In another case, an easy synthetic method was developed to produce metal nanoparticles in aqueous solution via an ultrathin protein-bonding layer, which are superior than conventional methods using high pressure and high temperature [121].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and surface roughness of nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and surface roughness of nanomaterials affect protein aggregation by influencing protein adsorption and diffusion. For example, amyloidogenic proteins are more likely to be adsorbed on the surface of irregularly shaped nanorods or nanostars than on nanospheres [122]. The adsorption of protein to a relatively flat nanomaterial surface will increase lateral interaction and enhance protein adsorption capacity [123,124] (Fig. 4E). Furthermore, the adsorbed proteins on nanomaterials affect aggregation by regulating the conformation and distribution of the protein in the system [125,126]. Moreover, since fibril growth requires a system with sufficient fluidity to enable protein diffusion, and compared with smooth surface, a rough surface slows down protein diffusion delaying/inhibiting fibrillation (Fig. 4E) [127–129]. Therefore, fine control of protein aggregation may be achieved by adjusting the surface morphology of nanomaterials.

Nanomaterials affect protein aggregation in a concentration dependent manner [130,131]. Lower nanomaterial-protein ratios are thought to lead to a higher surface protein concentration on the nanomaterial and that crowded surface environment may facilitate protein-protein interaction and accelerate fibrillation [125]. In contrast, increased nanomaterial-protein ratios may disrupt protein-protein interactions and block oligomerization/nucleation (Fig. 4B) [132]. In a study on the effect of polystyrene nanoparticles on Aβ aggregation, at a fixed protein concentration (8 μM), nanoparticles accelerate fibrillation at low particle concentration (1–17 mg/mL), while inhibiting fibrillation at higher concentrations (100–300 mg/mL) [88]. The opposite effects of the same nanomaterials on amyloid formation suggest the optimal concentration ratio between nanomaterials and protein should be thoroughly investigated for practical applications, and more critically, in both in vitro and in vivo settings.

Nanomaterials, with their unique properties including high surface to volume ratio, stable physical and chemical properties, good permeability, bioavailability and biocompatibility, are potential candidates for treating diseases like PMDs in non-invasive ways [133,134]. Although the results are encouraging, most studies to date have been performed in vitro. Herein, possible mechanisms and impact factors of nanomaterials inhibiting fibrillation are elucidated to help understand how nanomaterials regulate fibrillation. More experimental evidence is needed to demonstrate that the proposed mechanisms and optimal nanoparticle formulation has efficacy in both in vitro and in vivo settings.

Despite the excellent performance of various nanomaterials in inhibiting fibrillation in test tubes, the translational value of these results remains unclear. Unlike simplified in vitro studies, in vivo, nanomaterials tend to adsorb biomacromolecules, predominantly plasma proteins, after entering an organism to form a “protein corona” complex [135]. The formation of the protein corona may change the physicochemical properties of the nanomaterial, such as shape, size, and conformation of adsorbed proteins, thus impacting its therapeutic effects [114,136]. Therefore, future investigations on the interactions between nanomaterials and plasma/cell receptors, and the effects of the protein corona, will be required to fully understand the mechanisms of nano-inhibitors in vivo and to assure their safety and efficacy. Primary tasks include enhancing the targeting and selectivity of nanomaterials, optimizing bio-degradable and low-toxic nanoparticle formulation, and these approaches all will lead to the development of effective nanomaterials at lower dosages with great safety. The success of these investigations heavily relies on reliable in vivo models, so far, mostly used in vivo models are rodents, zebrafish, and Caenorhabditis elegans [137,138]. Among them, zebrafish, due to its availability in large numbers, optical transparency, and low costs, is a good preclinical model to help design/optimize nanomedicine and to better understand their working mechanisms [139]. Meanwhile, more in vivo attempts should be made in large animals such as dogs and monkeys.
S.L.W., J.Z., L.M., R.B.P., L.X. and K.H. prepared the review.
